skill.name: biomni
skill.description: Biomedical research skill for using Biomni to plan, run, and document multi-step analyses across genomics, drug discovery, clinical genomics, and literature synthesis.
skill.summary: Use Biomni for autonomous biomedical analysis workflows with integrated databases and LLM-guided code execution.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many hits, and most Markdown backtick, weak-crypto, C2, and hardcoded-URL detections are false positives from documentation examples. However, the skill intentionally supports autonomous LLM-generated code execution, external MCP tools, local file writes, and API key storage, so it should not be published without strong warnings and sandbox guidance.

high_findings.title: Autonomous Unsandboxed Code Execution
high_findings.description: The skill describes Biomni as generating and executing analysis code, with code execution enabled by default and sandbox mode disabled unless configured. This is a real high-risk capability because LLM-generated biomedical workflows can execute with the user process privileges.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly states that Biomni generates and executes code and warns that it runs with full system privileges. This confirms the static execution findings as a real security concern, although not malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Interactive Credential Collection and Persistence
high_findings.description: The setup helper prompts users for LLM provider API keys and can write them into a local .env file. This is legitimate setup behavior, but it increases exposure if run in shared directories or logs are mishandled.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly prompts for named API keys and writes collected values to .env or prints shell export commands. The behavior is clear and intentional, but it is configuration support rather than exfiltration.

high_findings.title: External MCP Command and Network Tool Integration
high_findings.description: The skill documents MCP server configuration that can run external commands and pass API keys into network-enabled tools. This expands the execution and network surface when users enable MCP integrations.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows documented command-based MCP servers and API key environment mappings. This is optional and user-configured, so confidence is high for risk surface but lower for immediate exploitability.

medium_findings.title: Subprocess-Based Setup and Test Execution
medium_findings.description: The setup script runs conda commands and can create then execute a generated Biomni test script. The subprocess arguments are mostly fixed, so command injection evidence was not found, but running the test can trigger package behavior, data access, and LLM calls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess.run calls are real and the generated test file is executed. The arguments are not shell-concatenated user input, which reduces injection likelihood.

medium_findings.title: Unescaped HTML Report Rendering
medium_findings.description: The report generator inserts conversation content and titles into HTML without escaping. If untrusted conversation history is converted to HTML and opened in a browser, embedded markup may execute or alter the report.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The converter wraps raw message content into HTML tags without escaping. Exploitability depends on the report input source and how users open generated HTML.

medium_findings.title: Local Filesystem Writes for Reports, Examples, and Secrets
medium_findings.description: The skill writes reports, example scripts, data directories, test files, and .env credential files. These writes are user-facing and mostly expected, but they can expose sensitive research data or credentials if paths are shared.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and legitimate for setup and reporting. The main risk is accidental disclosure of credentials or biomedical data, not hidden persistence.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Examples Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command detections in SKILL.md and references are Markdown fenced code examples, inline model names, or biomedical examples. I did not find evidence that these documentation snippets execute by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced documentation examples rather than Ruby backtick execution. Similar static hits across reference files follow the same Markdown pattern.

low_findings.title: Biomedical Text Misclassified as Crypto or C2 Indicators
low_findings.description: Static blocker hits for weak cryptography and C2 keywords appear to match biomedical abbreviations, model identifiers, SMILES strings, or example task text. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation, command-and-control behavior, or malicious networking.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected context shows domain content and documentation, not cryptographic code or C2 logic. This supports dismissing those static blocker patterns as false positives.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Provider Links
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to provider consoles, project documentation, GitHub resources, and local example endpoints. No evidence found that the skill silently sends data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visibly documentation, provider setup, or localhost examples. Network access can occur when users configure providers, but these lines do not show covert exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: LLM-Generated Code Execution Enabled by Default
dangerous_patterns.description: The API reference shows code execution enabled by default and sandbox mode disabled unless separately configured.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The configuration example directly states the default execution and sandbox settings. This is a concrete dangerous pattern even for legitimate research workflows.

dangerous_patterns.title: MCP Configuration Can Launch External Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The MCP configuration example defines command and args entries for external tools and maps API keys into their environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The JSON example is documentation, but it describes a supported runtime capability that can execute external tools. Exploitability depends on user-supplied MCP configuration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plaintext .env Credential Storage
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup helper can save collected provider keys into a local .env file in the current working directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The implementation explicitly writes each collected key and value to .env. This is intentional configuration behavior with clear credential exposure risk.

content.value_statement: Biomedical research workflows often require many tools, databases, and analysis steps. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use Biomni for structured genomics, drug discovery, and clinical analysis tasks.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup and use of the Biomni A1 agent for biomedical research tasks.
Provides examples for CRISPR screening, single-cell RNA-seq, GWAS, ADMET, and clinical genomics.
Documents configuration for Anthropic, OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, Google Gemini, Groq, AWS Bedrock, and custom endpoints.
Includes helper scripts for environment setup and report generation from Biomni conversation histories.
Explains MCP server integration for optional external biomedical tools and search providers.
Shows how to save conversation history and structure reproducible research reports.
limitations: Biomni can execute LLM-generated code and should be run in an isolated environment.
Biomedical results require expert review before clinical, laboratory, or regulatory decisions.
Large data downloads, provider API keys, and third-party model access may be required.
The helper scripts are examples and may need hardening for shared or production systems.

use_cases.title: Design CRISPR Screening Workflows
use_cases.description: Plan guide libraries, controls, hit criteria, and interpretation steps for gene editing studies.

use_cases.title: Analyze Omics Datasets
use_cases.description: Structure single-cell, GWAS, transcriptomics, and multi-omics analyses with clear inputs and expected outputs.

use_cases.title: Prepare Biomedical Research Reports
use_cases.description: Convert Biomni conversations into documented reports that include tasks, methods, results, and caveats.

prompt_templates.title: Ask a Basic Gene Question
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Biomni to summarize the main functions of TP53. Include disease links, pathways, and key evidence sources.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a CRISPR Screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Biomni to design a CRISPR knockout screen for autophagy in HEK293 cells. Include controls, guide selection criteria, and hit analysis steps.

prompt_templates.title: Interpret GWAS Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Biomni to analyze type 2 diabetes GWAS summary statistics. Prioritize loci, map variants to genes, and propose pathway enrichment outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate Multi-Omics Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Biomni to integrate genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics evidence for Alzheimer disease. Identify drivers, pathways, and therapeutic targets.

output_examples.input: Design a CRISPR screen for genes regulating autophagy in HEK293 cells.
output_examples.output: A structured screen plan with target scope, control genes, guide design criteria, quality checks, hit thresholds, and follow-up validation ideas.

output_examples.input: Analyze single-cell RNA-seq data from human PBMC samples.
output_examples.output: A workflow covering quality control, normalization, clustering, cell type annotation, differential expression, visualizations, and interpretation notes.

output_examples.input: Create a Biomni report from a saved conversation history.
output_examples.output: A readable research report with task context, metadata, analysis sections, generated figures or file references, and reproducibility notes.

best_practices: Run Biomni in a container, VM, or restricted workspace before enabling code execution.
Keep API keys out of shared directories and rotate keys after experiments with sensitive data.
Review generated code, data sources, and biomedical claims before using results in decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not run autonomous analyses against sensitive patient data without privacy controls.
Do not treat Biomni output as medical advice or validated clinical interpretation.
Do not enable arbitrary MCP servers or custom endpoints without reviewing their commands and permissions.

faq.question: What is Biomni used for?
faq.answer: Biomni is used for multi-step biomedical research tasks across genomics, drug discovery, molecular biology, and clinical analysis.

faq.question: Does this skill run code?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation describes LLM-generated code execution, so users should run it in a restricted environment.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require API keys?
faq.answer: Yes. At least one supported LLM provider key is usually required for Biomni workflows.

faq.question: Can it be used for clinical diagnosis?
faq.answer: It can help analyze clinical genomics examples, but all outputs need expert clinical review.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The skill combines generated code execution, local file writes, API keys, network providers, and optional external tools.
